The Wolves could not afford to run it back again after making minimal changes to the group in the summer of 2025, especially after watching Towns win a championship in New York this season. Edwards wants to win right now, and the Wolves are banking on addition by subtraction, believing that removing RandleÆs ISO-heavy offensive game and redistributing his 15.3 shots per game to McDaniels and Reid will open things up for Minnesota in a different way.

McDaniels took a big step forward with his offense, averaging a career-high 14.8 points and shooting 41 percent from 3-point range. Barring another trade for a more proven star, McDaniels will assume the role of the No. 2 offensive option on the team behind Edwards. His increased playmaking and shot creation last season, coupled with a dominant playoff series against Denver, gave the Wolves hope that he is ready for a bigger role on a consistent basis.

Reid will give the Wolves more spacing than Randle did. He is a career 37 percent 3-point shooter, which will give the Wolves starting lineup much-needed spacing. Randle converted just 31 percent from deep last season, which combined with Rudy GobertÆs non-existent shooting to allow opposing defenses to load up on Edwards at the point of attack. ReidÆs shooting and quick decision-making should breathe some life into the Wolves offense and give Edwards a little bit more room to operate.

[Robbins] In recent weeks, Wizards decision-makers became convinced that, with the NBAÆs new anti-tanking measures compelling more teams to compete, Young was going to command maximum-salary contract offers from other franchises through either a straight free-agent signing or a sign-and-trade
https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/7382866/2026/06/22/trae-young-washington-wizards-new-contract/?unlocked_article_code=1.sFA.076j.y1Obl2kdWXlP&source=athletic_user_shared_gift_article_copylink&smid=url-share-ta

The NBA rumor mill, which is often inaccurate, predicted months ago that any new deal between Young and the Wizards would average no more than $40 million annually.

But in recent weeks, Wizards decision-makers became convinced that, with the NBAÆs new anti-tanking measures compelling more teams to compete, Young was going to command maximum-salary contract offers from other franchises through either a straight free-agent signing or a sign-and-trade proposal.

Because the Wizards hold YoungÆs Bird rights, the Wizards had the latitude to offer Young up to a five-year contract with 8 percent annual raises. But YoungÆs new four-year deal instead features 5 percent year-over-year raises, the maximum year-over-year raise that any other team could have offered Young as a non-Bird free agent. For Washington, the difference between signing Young to 5 percent raises instead of 8 percent raises will amount to a total savings of $8.9 million over four years.

Wizards officials are not concerned that YoungÆs new contract will age poorly and prevent them from making future moves to improve their roster. Anthony Davis, who is due to earn $58.5 million in 2026-27, and Young are now WashingtonÆs highest-paid players on its young roster, but the person with the third-largest salary is big man Alex Sarr, who will be paid the relatively small sum of $12.3 million this season. At the earliest, the Wizards do not expect to approach the dreaded first apron until the 2028-29 season, when any new rookie-scale contract extensions for Sarr and Kyshawn George would go into effect.

Plus, Wizards officials reason that Young, who will turn 28 years old in September, will remain in his prime years through the end of his contract. The onerous large contracts that age the worst Ś potentially Jimmy ButlerÆs current deal with the Golden State Warriors and Paul GeorgeÆs current deal with the Philadelphia 76ers, for example Ś tend to be contracts in which players already are past their primes at the start of their contracts.

At the same time, though, Wizards officials have always known that their lineups would have to feature enough positional size and enough defensive-oriented players to compensate for YoungÆs shortcomings Ś in the same way that the defensive liabilities of LaMelo Ball, Jalen Brunson, Luka Don?i?, Kyrie Irving and Donovan Mitchell (and others) are compensated for by their respective teams.

The WizardsÆ decision-makers believe Davis and youngsters Bilal Coulibaly, Davis, George, Sarr and whomever they pick first overall in Tuesday nightÆs draft will develop into strong enough defenders to help Young.

Observation: Only 9 of 37 seasons since 1990 had a completely undisputed best player
r/nba - Seasons since 1990
Seasons since 1990
I used a very conservative definition of "consensus best player." To count as the consensus best player, the person had to (all in the same season):

Win regular season MVP

Win the championship

Win finals MVP

Be the obvious best player in the world at the time without any material debate

Since 1990 there have been 37 seasons. The year listed is the year of the playoffs. So 1990 refers to the 1989-1990 season. There are only 9 times where a player achieved everything in the same season.

The most noteworthy (or questionable) exception is Hakeem in 1994, when he did win everything, but I'm not counting him because of Jordan playing baseball, and because nobody at the time considered Hakeem to be on Jordan's level.

Bill Russell was the first Black MVP, All-NBA first teamer, head coach and champion coach, and led the NBA's first all-Black starting lineup to a 12-0 record. Yet he declined being the first Black Hall of Famer, believing earlier Black pioneers who didn't get a chance in the pros deserved the honor
He was also the joint-first Black NCAA champion in 1955.

Russell declined to attend his initial Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame induction ceremony in 1975. The Boston Celtics legend refused to be the first Black player inducted, believing that others who came before him deserved the honor first.  

The first black player enshrined instead was Charles Cooper https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Cooper_(basketball) (at the behest of Russell), who helped his team (the New York Rens) win 88 straight games in the 1930s, a record that has never been matched by a professional basketball team. Technically he did play professionally, but he wasnÆt able to play in any professional league with white players, or to be a teammate of one.

They also won the 1939 World Basketball Championship, which was probably the best way that the best basketball team in the world was determined at that time, given that it included black teams like the Renaissance and the Globetrotters.

Notably, Russell also declined to get his jersey retired in front of fans in Boston, as he felt the most dominant dynasty in the sport's history was not appreciated because they had Black players.

This feeling was exacerbated by his own fans very literally breaking into his house and shitting on his bed. When he retired suddenly in the summer of 1969 after his 11th ring, he all but cut ties with the organization for years.

On the court, Russell won 55 straight games and two national championships at a program that had not previously been a powerhouse, and 11 NBA championships in 13 years. From 1952-1984, Russell won 11 championships, and no other player won more than three, excluding seasons where those players were on Bill Russell's team.

Players who has the most top 5 mvp finishes in a row in nba history
I wanted to make it ōin a rowö rather than total because I wanted to see how many years in a row a player could be a durable and healthy, a top 5 player in the world, and their team have a sustainable record in order to be in mvp talks.

1st - LeBron James, 2006 - 2018 (13 years)

LeBron was top 5 in mvp voting for 13 years in a row, and has a total of 14 top 5 finishes, becoming an mvp candidate again in 2020. The only reason he doesnÆt have 15 in a row is because in 2019 the lakers had a losing record and they failed to make the playoffs.

2nd - Kareem Abdul-Jabbar, 1970 - 1981 (12 years)

Kareem actually has the most top 5 mvp finishes in nba history with 15, he fell out of those talks in 1982 and 1983. The last time he was top 5 in mvp voting he was 38 years old.

3rd - Bill Russell, 1958 - 1967 (10 years)

Bill Russell in his sophomore year (just like Kareem) won mvp. He was all nba second team when he won 3 of his mvps, and he was all nba first team for 2 of them.

4th - A two way tie

Magic Johnson, 1982 - 1991 (9 years)

Larry Bird, 1980 - 1988 (9 years)

Magic and Bird were also both all-nba first team every single year that they were also top 5 in mvp voting

5th - A three way tie this time

Tim Duncan, 1998 - 2005 (8 years)

Kobe Bryant, 2006 - 2013 (8 years)

Oscar Robertson, 1961 - 1968 (8 years)

Time Duncan was top 5 in mvp voting one other time in 2007 and then never again.

Kobe Bryant was top 5 in mvp voting for 3 years before he missed it in 2005. This was a year he was below .500 and didnÆt garner a single mvp vote.

Oscar Robertson surprised me the most, but also wasnÆt very surprising. He was top 5 in mvp voting again in 1971 (the year he won a chip with Kareem and the bucks) and then never again.

The current player with the most top 5 finishes in a row (minus LeBron) is Giannis with 7, though he broke his streak this year.

ThatÆs the list, I wasnÆt very surprised by it but it is cool to see nonetheless. One thing I wanna mention is that Michael Jordan isnÆt on this list because I wanted to show sustained excellence. No doubt he wouldÆve been top 5 in mvp voting if he stayed in the league in 1994 and æ95, but he didnÆt. If I donÆt include those years he would be tied with Bill Russell, but I donÆt want to be biased.

I think that Magic and Bird being tied is pretty cool, but thatÆs my off season stats post, hope you enjoyed the read.

How each NBA team assembled their current rosters Visualized
A few months ago I found out that the Timberwolves were able to acquire the draft rights to Jaden McDaniels in part because of the 2007 Trade of Kevin Garnett to the Celtics. It made me wonder, could you visualize how an NBA team assembled their entire roster just by charting out Draft picks, Trades, and Free Agency Signings? This question started a months long journey of compiling the information and then making family tree style visualizations for each team. About a week ago I posted a number of these in the various team subreddits and received a lot of very helpful feedback and a number of people urging me to post all of the trees in the main nba sub. Below is the result of this hobby project that has taken up a majority of my free time in the back half of this season.

Timberwolves

FAQ:

This trade doesn't show every team that was involved

The trades are presented from the perspective of the team being shown. For example, in the Kevin Durant trade to the Rockets, there were 7 teams involved, but not every team sent a pick or a player to every other team. So from the perspective of the Rockets, the only teams that sent them anything are the hawks and suns, so they are the only teams shown.

I don't see X trade that I know this team made

These trade trees do not cover every transaction by a team, but instead only the draft picks/free agent signings/trades that are still providing the team value. I also elected not to show how the teams acquired each of their future picks because it was complicated enough already and instead I decided to just show which draft picks the team has in the "Future draft picks" Box

Lost in the Knicks winning the NBA Championship last week: Jalen Brunson (2,240) has passed Kyrie Irving (2,211) in total career playoff points. He is just 30 away from passing Giannis Antetokounmpo (2,270)
After dropping 45 in the Game 5 closeout to clinch the title in San Antonio, Jalen Brunson has climbed the all-time playoff scoring list, passing Kyrie Irving and other players along the way.

Brunson now sits at 2,240, tied with Hall of Famer Bob Pettit, and trails right behind Donovan Mitchell (2,251), Isiah Thomas (2,261) and Giannis Antetokounmpo (2,270) just ahead of him on the list.

In this playoff run alone he passed Allen Iverson, Moses Malone, David Robinson, Gary Payton, George Gervin, Jason Kidd and Steve Nash.

Brunson also now ranks 3rd on the KnicksÆ all-time playoff scoring list with 1,796 points.

If the Knicks remain contenders and keep this core together, Brunson should be able to keep climbing.

When the NBA was formed via merger in 1949, eight of nine NBL teams entered the new NBA. The ninth, the all-Black, Black-owned Dayton Rens, was excluded. After inheriting the NBLÆs worst record midseason, the Rens had a playoff-caliber mark, but the NBA cited the inherited record to disqualify them
To clarify (ran out of characters), the Rens took on the worst team's record midseason, which was 2-17. In other words, they were 2-17 before actually playing a game. The NBA's first commissioner, Maurice Podoloff, and the NBL, cited that they had the worst record in the league, and used that justification to exclude them.

They only had the worst record because they inherited that other team's record (in a 59 game season), and in the games in which they played they had a winning percentage good enough to make the playoffs.

This was despite half of their team having to leave to barnstorm due to a lack of financial support in what, at the time, was a deeply racist and segregated city in Dayton, Ohio. An estimated 1/5 of Daytonians at that time were a part of the KKK.

For the league's first season, instead of having an all-Black team, there were no Black players in the league. The Rens were arguably the best team in history up to that point, at one point winning 88 straight games and multiple world championships, but were repeatedly undercut by corruption, possibly rigged games, refusing to play into stereotypes of the time period, and Podoloff, who the MVP trophy was named after until 2022. A confluence of these factors forced them to dissolve within the next two years.

If you're interested in background on the situation, as well as a very different early history of the Harlem Globetrotters than what is often portrayed, here's a much more detailed post: https://np.reddit.com/r/nba/comments/1u945h0/oc_the_new_yorkdayton_renaissance_the_littleknown/

Amateur Scouting Accuracy, League History
At this time of year, a lot of people are obsessing about the order of draft picks, and scouting reports on amateur players, and so forth.  This post is for those people, to potentially help them relax a bit, and get less wound up over their team picking at number whatever, instead of number whatever plus x.

It turns out that scouting 18 to 20 year old kids, and trying to determine which ones will excel as professional basketball players and which ones won't, is tremendously difficult, and a science that the basketball industry has made little progress on in well-nigh a century now.

Using an imperfect, but objective, measure like win-shares, we find that it is very rare for the NBA to successfully identify the top draft prospects.

There is, in fact, only one season in which all three of the best players were drafted within the top three picks in a draft:  ( drafts in the last two decades obviously have incomplete data as those players remain active )

Draft Year	Top Pick	WS rank	Second Pick	WS rank	Third Pick	WS rank
1982	James Worthy	3	Terry Cummings	2	Dominique Wilkins	1
Even more amazingly, there are only 14 seasons in which even two of the top three players were drafted within the top 3 picks: ( in parentheses, draft pick position, then eventual WS rank in draft class )

2007     Kevin Durant (2->1), Al Horford (3->2)
2003     LeBron James (1->1), Carmelo Anthony (3->3)
2001     Pau Gasol (3->1), Tyson Chandler (2->3)
1997     Tim Duncan (1->1), Chauncey Billups (3->2)
1994     Jason Kidd (2->1), Grant Hill (3->2)
1993     Chris Webber (1->2), Anfrenee Hardaway (3->3)
1992     Shaquille O'Neal (1->1), Alonzo Mourning (2->3)
1990     Gary Payton (2->1), Derrick Coleman (1->2)
1981     Buck Williams (3->1), Isiah Thomas(2->3)
1968     Elvin Hayes (1->1), Wes Unseld (2->2)
1967     Earl Monroe (2->2), Jimmy Walker (1->3)
1960     Oscar Robertson (1->1), Jerry West (2->2)
1959     Wilt Chamberlain (1->1), Bailey Howell(2->2)
1957     Jim Krebs (3->2), Charlie Tyra(2->3)
Outcome	Number of Occurrences	Last occurrence
All three best players drafted in the top 3	1	1982
2 of the best 3 drafted in the top 3	14	2007
1 of the best 3 drafted in top 3	34	2025
Zero of the best 3 drafted in the top 3	22	2024
Picks 1-2 winding up the 2 best players	4	1990
( Methodology note: I skipped the first half-dozen drafts, as territorial picks muddy the waters too much. )

There are a few conclusions to draw here;

It is rare (21%) that even two of the top three picks wind up becoming among the three-best players from a draft.

In 31% of drafts, __none__ of the top three picks wind up among the three-best players from that draft.

If anything, scouting accuracy is declining -- most likely due to the fact that teams rarely wait sufficiently long to see players play against adult opponents in college.

The only 14 first picks to actually have the best career, by this objective measure, are; Karl-Anthony Towns, Anthony Davis, LeBron James, Dwight Howard, Tim Duncan, David Robinson, Earvin Johnson, Bob Lanier, Kareem Abdul-Jabbar, Elvin Hayes, Walt Bellamy, Elgin Baylor, Oscar Robertson, and Wilt Chamberlain.

It is 22 times more likely that all three of the top three picks in a draft will disappoint than it is that none of them will.

Regardless of who is drafted with the first three picks in this upcoming draft, it is very likely that at least one and possibly all three will disappoint, with respect to their draft position.

Finally, everyone relax.

Have fun at the draft, y'all.

Post-script corollary to the note above about first-picks who actually had the best career from the draft: it seems that if you aren't picking the top center available (11 times), or a big guard with unheard-of skills (3 times), you are not going to wind up with your #1 overall being the best player.

[OC] The NBA Championship Belt (2025-26 Edition)
Original Content
Many longtime members of the subreddit will be familiar with the idea of the NBA Championship Belt. The concept is simple: If you beat the team with the Belt, you now own the Belt. You lose it if you lose a game and the team that beats you then keeps it until they lose.

If the team with the Belt is a playoff team, the Belt will ultimately end up with the team that wins the Finals. Then it should be no surprise that the New York Knicks are the current wearers of the Belt. The season usually ends up with the NBA Champion wearing the Belt, but it doesn't always end up that way.

The most recent example of the exception happened when the Phoenix Suns, owners of the Belt at the time, faced off against the Sacramento Kings in what was both of the teams' last game of the 2021-22 season. The Suns were the 1 seed out West with 64 wins, and the Kings had no chance of making the playoffs as is often the case. Because both teams' destinies were already decided, they rested some guys, and the shorthanded Kings were able to beat the shorter-handed Suns in the last game of the season. Because the Kings season was over, no one had a chance to steal the Belt from them for the remainder of the season, so they held it for the entire offseason.

Rules of the Belt
Earlier I stated that the Belt was a simple concept and that there was just one rule. Of course, that rule is the most important, but there are a few distinctions to make.

First, I decided to include all regular season, playoff, play-in, and in-season tournament games as part of the Belt's history. If a team loses a preseason or Summer League game, they get to keep the Belt. If this weren't the case, it would be possible for the Belt to end up overseas, and while it would be interesting to follow the Belt's lineage in that scenario, the differences in documentation for each league would make scraping the data a nightmare.

Another rule is that I started at the 1949-50 season. Technically, the NBA says its first season was the 1946-47 BAA season, before it merged with the NBL, but I disagree with the NBA's removal of NBL history, so I started it where I think it should be. I did let the Lakers, winners of the 1949 BAA championship, start with the Belt, to at least give a little credence to the leagueÆs opinion. This ends up not mattering in the long run since no matter what team starts with the Belt, it ends up converging to the same path in under a season.

Last rule. Some people start the season by automatically giving the Belt to the team that won the Finals the prior season. I don't think this is as fun, because a team like the Kings getting the moral victory of holding the Belt for the entire offseason is a much more interesting talking point. So, I will not be using this rule, however, as I mentioned earlier, it doesn't really matter in the long run as paths converge quite quickly.

Cade Cunningham (20 games): 27/6/8, 4.8 TOV, 43% FG, 40% 3P, won 12 of 20 games (60%).

Nickeil Alexander-Walker (6 games): 24/4/3, 2 TOV, 54% FG, 50% 3P, won 3 of 6 games (50%).

Donovan Mitchell (14 games): 27/5/4, 43% FG, 29% 3P, won 4 of 14 games (29%).

Victor Wembanyama (9 games): 22/11/3, 3.9 BLK, 3 TOV, 41% FG, 24% 3P, won 4 of 9 games (44%).

SGA and Brunson are your two best candidates for the Belt MVP here, although MPJ and NAW both see their production drastically improve in belt games. Another interesting player in Belt games is Russell Westbrook, who only played in 4 belt games, but averaged 21/5/7 on 51% from the field and 51% from 3.

Closing Remarks
I have stats like this for the entirety of NBA history, so if that's something you're interested in seeing in an additional post, do let me know in the comments. Thanks for reading.

Victor Wembanyama in 14 games has the highest playoff EPM of all time at +10.5.
Estimated plus minus is considered either the most reliable or 2nd most reliable publically available advanced stat by general managers depending on the poll. The second highest estimated plus minus is Karl Anthony Towns with a very healthy +6.8. The highest EPM playoff runs (beyond 10 games) is LeBron James in 2009 with 14 games and a +9.4, and Kawai Leonard in 2017 with 11 games and +9.4. Wemby is 14 games in (12 full games being fair) with a 10.5 (and was still 9.7 after last game, his worst in the series).

Obviously I am not suggesting that Wemby is literally having the best playoff run in history, and he could obviously underperform in the following games and this could drop heavily. I certainly would say there are still several playoff runs I would confidently put above it at this point. ThereÆs so many factors that go into stats like these, and the sheer volume of the net swings indicates that the team is built around WembyÆs minutes to dominate, which definitely skews the stat to some extent.

But itÆs not hyperbole that Wemby is having one of the greatest postseason runs since 2000 thus far, and while that could easily change for the worst, itÆs still a remarkable feat 14 games in. And the box plus minus stats certainly back this up. Wemby is +50 in 4 games this conference finals while the Spurs are +4, thatÆs a 96 point swing (as the player playing the most minutes on the Spurs) in 4 games! Heæs won his minutes all but two games since February 1st, and all but one game where heÆs played more than 50 minutes. While this could easily change and Wemby could struggle in following games, thus far his impact has been absolutely enormous. To me Wemby has an extremely reasonable case as the best player in the world right now, and has been the clear best player of the past 3-4 months and this postseason even if Jokic and SGA had better overall regular seasons.

Victor Wembanyama has 3 blocks titles in 3 years. The all-time record is 4
Looks like Wemby is likely to become the joint all-time leader in blocks titles next year at the ripe old age of 23.

As it stands, Kareem, Mark Eaton and Marcus Camby are tied for most blocks titles with 4 each.

Wemby is tied with AD, Mutombo, Hakeem, Theo Ratliff and George T Johnson for 4th on the list with 3 each.

He surpasses this season the likes of Dwight Howard, Alonzo Mourning and Manute Bol.

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_NBA_annual_blocks_leaders

Blocks have been officially tracked since 1973-74.

For those wondering how Wemby could lead the league in blocks per game last year despite playing less than the 58 games required to qualify for league leaders, there is actually a rule the NBA put in place in 2013-14 which allows it. A playerÆs total is divided by 58 instead of however many games they played, and that average (which represents what they would have averaged per game had they suited up and recorded no stats for however many games they were short of 58) is the one considered for the leaderboards.

Wemby only played 46 games last year, but still led the league in total blocks at 176. 176/58 = 3.03 which is still more than anyone else averaged.

Jokic and Giannis have nearly identical basketball resumes. Why is there such a huge gap in perception and all-time ranking between the two?
Source: https://www.basketball-reference.com/players/a/antetgi01.html (Stats from 2018-19 to today - the first year they both finished top 5 in MVP voting)

Giannis Antetokounmpo (29.4/11.8/6.0 on 63.1% TS): 1x Championship, 1x Finals MVP, 2x MVP, 1x DPOY, 9x All-NBA, 10x All-Star, 5x All-Defense, 1x MIP, various other accolades (1x ASG MVP, 1x NBA Cup, 1x NBA Cup MVP)

Nikola Jokic (25.1/11.8/8.7 on 64.9% TS): 1x Championship, 1x Finals MVP, 3x MVP, 7x All-NBA, 8x All-Star

Jokic has 1 more MVP, but Giannis has a DPOY, 5x All-Defense, and both more All-NBA and All-Star selections. Jokic is a superior playmaker and triple double machine with historic offensive impact but heÆs a negative defender. Giannis is a 2-way wrecking ball who gives you 30/12/7 on historic efficiency while also being a DPOY and all time great defender.

Both rose to superstardom at the same time and peaked in the exact same era. Both have had to carry flawed supporting casts, both only made it out of the second round twice, both only have one ring that involved a good amount of luck.

Their careers actually mirror each other to a shocking degree. Both have:

- multiple MVPs in the same era

- only 2x making it past the second round: one championship run (2021, 2023) and a WCF loss to the eventual champ (2019, 2020)

- multiple runs where they lost due to injuries that shouldnÆt be used to criticize them (2022-25 for Giannis, 2021/22 for Jokic)

- flawed supporting casts headlined by a homegrown scorer/shot creator (Murray/Middleton) and a defensive anchor (AG/Jrue)

- glaring weaknesses that shape team building but didnÆt stop them from being all time great MVP level players (Giannis 3pt shooting, Jokic defense)

- both were underrated prospects who developed over time and blossomed into superstars by the end of the 2010s

TheyÆre equal in my eyes, and I have no issue if fans prefer one over the other as itÆs mostly a preference of offense vs. defense. My issue is with a large portion of fans declaring Jokic to be in a completely separate tier higher than Giannis all time when thatÆs not reasonable given their accomplishments.

Why do so many people, especially Bill Simmons, Zach Lowe, the Ringer folk, etc. as well as people on here rank Jokic so much higher? IÆve seen hot takes where people rank Jokic above Bird, Magic, Hakeem, Shaq, etc. while Giannis feels almost forgotten in all time convos.

The best offensive seasons ever look different when you grade value per touch. HereÆs the top 20.
IÆve been working on a box-score-only way to measure how much value a player creates every time they actually touch the ball. Not an impact stat, not a catch-all, and not something meant to replace anything. ItÆs just a clean way to look at offensive engine value using public data and nothing else.

One nice thing about grading offense per touch is that the results mostly confirm what people already understand. The best creators rise to the top no matter what era you look at, and the behavior lines up with how modern impact metrics treat high-level offensive engines. It also ends up capturing what people generally want out of a box-score offensive metric without needing any black-box assumptions or era-specific regression quirks. ItÆs basically a straightforward, transparent way to quantify lead-creator value, and it holds up across eras.

Here are the top 20 seasons since 1978 using that approach:

Rank	Season	Player	TVR+
1	2016	Stephen Curry	162.2
2	2013	LeBron James	160.7
3	2015	Stephen Curry	160.4
4	2023	Nikola Jokic	158.3
5	2009	Chris Paul	157.7
6	1987	Magic Johnson	156.6
7	1989	Michael Jordan	155.8
8	2008	Chris Paul	155.7
9	2025	Nikola Jokic	155.7
10	2014	LeBron James	155.5
11	2010	LeBron James	153.3
12	1990	Magic Johnson	152.9
13	2022	Nikola Jokic	152.7
14	2013	Kevin Durant	152.6
15	1989	Magic Johnson	152.4
16	2016	Chris Paul	152.2
17	2012	Chris Paul	151.8
18	2012	LeBron James	151.8
19	1988	Larry Bird	151.6
20	2021	Nikola Jokic	151.6
A few things stand out. CurryÆs peak is as ridiculous as it felt. Jokic shows up right alongside the all-time engine seasons. CP3Æs best years look insane in this framework. Magic, MJ and Bird translate cleanly across eras. Harden and Durant land exactly where youÆd expect once you scroll further down the list.

2017 provides a good example of how this metric behaves. The narratives that year were loud, so itÆs a clean test of what per-touch value actually looks like. Filtering to players with at least 1000 minutes gives you this at the top:

Rank	Player	TVR+
1	Kevin Durant	150.6
2	Isaiah Thomas	149.5
3	Stephen Curry	148.8
4	James Harden	148.4
...
...
...
| 12 | Russell Westbrook | 138.4 |

ThatÆs usually the part that surprises people. WestbrookÆs raw production was massive, but his per-touch value wasnÆt anywhere near the guys at the top. The metric punishes turnover friction, inefficient self-creation and possession diet. HeÆs also the first MVP since Moses Malone in 1983 to finish outside the top 10 in per-touch value. Nobody else in the modern era comes close to that gap between narrative production and actual engine value.

According to NBA.com, none of the last 20 NBA championship teams were ranked lower than 8th in Net Rating
Of the past 20 NBA championship teams, only 7 had the best net rating, 3 had the best offensive rating, and 3 had the best defensive rating. So this isn't really particularly predictive of championship success.

However, none of them had a net rating lower than 8th (and only the 2011 Mavs were lower than 6th). According to that logic, the following teams are "contenders": Oklahoma City Thunder (12.6), Cleveland Cavaliers (11.1), Boston Celtics (8.8), Memphis Grizzlies (6.0), Denver Nuggets (5.2), New York Knicks (4.6), Houston Rockets (4.0), and Minnesota Timberwolves (3.0). We'll see where things land when we get to the end of the regular season.

Interestingly the lowest regular season NETRTG for any of the last 20 championship teams was the 2022-23 Denver Nuggets at 3.3. The top NETRTG that season was the Boston Celtics at only 6.7, the lowest of any top NETRTG in this span.
